articles.title: Amid confusion over Pfizer's emergency penicillin program, newborn is diagnosed with preventable syphilis
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus A STAT Investigation Amid confusion over Pfizer’s emergency penicillin program, newborn is diagnosed with preventable syphilis Rise in adult cases and Bicillin shortage set stage for ’never event’ Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Camille MacMillin/STAT By Eric Boodman June 15, 2026 For this story, Boodman obtained email chains between Pfizer and health officials and conducted interviews with 10 state health departments, eight local health agencies, six health system pharmacists, and former and current CDC officials, as well as infectious disease specialists, OB-GYNs, and representatives of Pfizer and Cost Plus Drugs, among other sources. Eric Boodman eric.boodman@statnews.com Eric Boodman focuses on narrative features, exploring the startling ways that science and medicine affect people’s lives. You can reach Eric on Signal at eboodman.88. Get email alerts for Eric Boodman Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts The request was an emergency. In late March, a woman in Gila County, Arizona, was diagnosed with syphilis, and she was pregnant. She needed an injection of penicillin — if possible, 30 days before delivery — but the bacteria corkscrewing through her body increased her risk of delivering early. Without timely treatment, her pregnancy could end in miscarriage, stillbirth, or infant death, and if the infant survived, the child might live with bone deformities, brain damage, blindness, and deafness, among other complications. All of that could be prevented with one of the oldest antibiotics in the book — specifically, an injectable form of penicillin sold under the brand name Bicillin L-A. Advertisement It was the only treatment approved in the United States for syphilis during pregnancy, and there’d been a national shortage since July 2025. But Pfizer, the only company that sells it, had a protocol in place for exactly this scenario: an emergency request system “to be used for confirmed congenital and risk of congenital syphilis patients only.” That was what was unfolding in Gila County — and on Friday, March 27, public health officials submitted a request. Pfizer confirmed receipt the next Monday, according to emails obtained by STAT. But over a week after that, on April 7, the medication still hadn’t arrived. “What can be done to get bicillin for this patient ASAP? These are the kind of delays that make the emergency request line not a viable option for public health response,” a leader at the National Coalition of STD Directors wrote to a Pfizer representative, stepping in to help secure the drug. Advertisement “Did they submit a medical request form first? I checked with Customer Service and they can’t locate. If we can get this medical request we will get it processed,” the Pfizer representative wrote back that same day. But by the time this exchange was taking place, it was already too late. “Mom has delivered and we have missed our opportunity to prevent congenital syphilis,” wrote an official at the Arizona Department of Health Services, adding, “Yes, the county completed the medical request form.” It was the outcome everyone was trying to avert. In February, the National Coalition of STD Directors had asked Pfizer to donate a fraction of its Bicillin L-A reserves to state health departments, so they’d have it on hand as a backstop in case this kind of emergency arose. But Pfizer hadn’t — and in early June, nearly four months after the coalition’s proposal, the company said it was still evaluating the idea of proactively sending out doses. This case in Arizona was a realization of the fears that had spurred the suggestion in the first place. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. Subscribe Log In Pfizer , pharma , public health , reproductive health Submit a correction request Reprints Eric Boodman General Assignment Reporter Eric Boodman focuses on narrative features, exploring the startling ways that science and medicine affect people’s lives. You can reach Eric on Signal at eboodman.88. STAT Plus A STAT Investigation
STAT Plus
A STAT Investigation
Amid confusion over Pfizer’s emergency penicillin program, newborn is diagnosed with preventable syphilis Rise in adult cases and Bicillin shortage set stage for ’never event’
Amid confusion over Pfizer’s emergency penicillin program, newborn is diagnosed with preventable syphilis
Rise in adult cases and Bicillin shortage set stage for ’never event’
Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article
Manage alerts for this article
Email this article
Share this article
Camille MacMillin/STAT By Eric Boodman June 15, 2026 For this story, Boodman obtained email chains between Pfizer and health officials and conducted interviews with 10 state health departments, eight local health agencies, six health system pharmacists, and former and current CDC officials, as well as infectious disease specialists, OB-GYNs, and representatives of Pfizer and Cost Plus Drugs, among other sources. Eric Boodman eric.boodman@statnews.com Eric Boodman focuses on narrative features, exploring the startling ways that science and medicine affect people’s lives. You can reach Eric on Signal at eboodman.88. Get email alerts for Eric Boodman Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts The request was an emergency. In late March, a woman in Gila County, Arizona, was diagnosed with syphilis, and she was pregnant. She needed an injection of penicillin — if possible, 30 days before delivery — but the bacteria corkscrewing through her body increased her risk of delivering early. Without timely treatment, her pregnancy could end in miscarriage, stillbirth, or infant death, and if the infant survived, the child might live with bone deformities, brain damage, blindness, and deafness, among other complications. All of that could be prevented with one of the oldest antibiotics in the book — specifically, an injectable form of penicillin sold under the brand name Bicillin L-A. Advertisement It was the only treatment approved in the United States for syphilis during pregnancy, and there’d been a national shortage since July 2025. But Pfizer, the only company that sells it, had a protocol in place for exactly this scenario: an emergency request system “to be used for confirmed congenital and risk of congenital syphilis patients only.” That was what was unfolding in Gila County — and on Friday, March 27, public health officials submitted a request. Pfizer confirmed receipt the next Monday, according to emails obtained by STAT. But over a week after that, on April 7, the medication still hadn’t arrived. “What can be done to get bicillin for this patient ASAP? These are the kind of delays that make the emergency request line not a viable option for public health response,” a leader at the National Coalition of STD Directors wrote to a Pfizer representative, stepping in to help secure the drug. Advertisement “Did they submit a medical request form first? I checked with Customer Service and they can’t locate. If we can get this medical request we will get it processed,” the Pfizer representative wrote back that same day. But by the time this exchange was taking place, it was already too late. “Mom has delivered and we have missed our opportunity to prevent congenital syphilis,” wrote an official at the Arizona Department of Health Services, adding, “Yes, the county completed the medical request form.” It was the outcome everyone was trying to avert. In February, the National Coalition of STD Directors had asked Pfizer to donate a fraction of its Bicillin L-A reserves to state health departments, so they’d have it on hand as a backstop in case this kind of emergency arose. But Pfizer hadn’t — and in early June, nearly four months after the coalition’s proposal, the company said it was still evaluating the idea of proactively sending out doses. This case in Arizona was a realization of the fears that had spurred the suggestion in the first place. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. articles.title: In many South Asian American families, a child's autism diagnosis is a secret
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Opinion First Opinion In many South Asian American families, a child’s autism diagnosis is a secret Cultural norms and health system failures make it difficult for kids to access the care they need Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Indian children with autism after an awareness campaign event in 2013. In many South Asian spiritual ideologies, disability is framed as punishment for past wrongdoing and internalized as personal moral failure. Altaf Qadri/AP By Ritu Goel June 15, 2026 Goel is a double board-certified child, adolescent, and adult psychiatrist and founder of MindClaire PC. The CDC’s latest data show autism prevalence is 1 in 31 children in the United States. That is 16% higher than the previous estimate in 2020. It is the kind of number that should change how health systems allocate resources, how pediatricians screen, and how schools plan. But there is a population this number barely touches: South Asian American families, where an autism diagnosis is still, in many homes, a secret. Advertisement I know this because I have lived on both sides of it. I am a child and adolescent psychiatrist. I am also a South Asian woman and a mother. For years, I worked with families navigating complex childhood diagnoses at institutions like Johns Hopkins, Kennedy Krieger, and Kaiser Permanente. The pattern I saw in the clinic is the same one the research now confirms : South Asian children are diagnosed with developmental disabilities later than white peers. (On average , minority children are diagnosed roughly a year to a year and a half later than their white peers.) Their families access fewer services. And the primary barrier is not language, insurance, or geography. Altaf Qadri/AP By Ritu Goel June 15, 2026 Goel is a double board-certified child, adolescent, and adult psychiatrist and founder of MindClaire PC. The CDC’s latest data show autism prevalence is 1 in 31 children in the United States. That is 16% higher than the previous estimate in 2020. It is the kind of number that should change how health systems allocate resources, how pediatricians screen, and how schools plan. But there is a population this number barely touches: South Asian American families, where an autism diagnosis is still, in many homes, a secret. Advertisement I know this because I have lived on both sides of it. I am a child and adolescent psychiatrist. I am also a South Asian woman and a mother. For years, I worked with families navigating complex childhood diagnoses at institutions like Johns Hopkins, Kennedy Krieger, and Kaiser Permanente. The pattern I saw in the clinic is the same one the research now confirms : South Asian children are diagnosed with developmental disabilities later than white peers. (On average , minority children are diagnosed roughly a year to a year and a half later than their white peers.) Their families access fewer services. And the primary barrier is not language, insurance, or geography. articles.title: As the New World Screwworm Returns, Scientists Unite to Fight Back
articles.text:
Skip to main content News As the New World Screwworm Returns, Scientists Unite to Fight Back As local and federal authorities respond to the current outbreak of the flesh-eating blowfly, researchers look ahead to new approaches to keep New World screwworm at bay. Written by Shelby Bradford, PhD Shelby Bradford, PhD Shelby is an Assistant Editor for The Scientist . She earned her PhD from West Virginia University in immunology and microbiology and completed an AAAS Mass Media fellowship. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Jun 19, 2026 | 6 min read Save for Later A blowfly that lays its eggs in wounds of live animals poses major health and safety risks. Image credit: ©iStock.com, Lidya Elfa Sari PDF version Register for free to listen to this article Listen with   Speechify 0:00 6:00 Share I nsect pests like flies are a nuisance, but some, like the New World screwworm, have the potential to devastate cattle, a serious and costly bane to the agriculture industry. The New World screwworm is a type of blowfly that sniffs out and lays eggs in the wounds of living tissue, often livestock and companion animals. When the larvae emerge, they eat the surrounding flesh before falling out to mature in the soil, causing more injury that attracts more blowflies to the site in the meantime. Before mitigation efforts, New World screwworm led to more than $200 million in livestock losses every year in the US alone. 1 While strategies to eliminate New World screwworm populations have worked well in the past, today, researchers are exploring new genetic technologies and multidisciplinary approaches to better combat this devastating pest. Sterile Insects Kept New World Screwworm at Bay The New World screwworm is endemic to the warm regions of the Americas. To combat this devastating fly, scientists took advantage of the insect’s sexual behavior. 2-4 Male blowflies mate aggressively, while female blowflies only mate once. By sterilizing large numbers of flies to release them into the wild, researchers discovered that they could drive out the blowflies by causing large numbers of unsuccessful pairings. 5 Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Immunology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe “It was a brilliant idea,” said Maxwell Scott , an entomologist at North Carolina State University. Indeed, the US in coordination with Mexico and, later, central American countries drove the New World screwworm out of their territories down to Panama in the 1960s. There, a production facility has continued to churn out up to 100 million flies a week to maintain a barrier between Panama and Colombia. “So, it can be very effective, but it is a numbers game. You've got to outnumber the fertile flies in the area,” Scott said. However, in 2022, the barricade broke. Researchers said that it was likely a combination of many factors: Varying land use had introduced more cattle into the area, the COVID-19 pandemic caused a decrease in the number of people available to conduct investigations, and the number of flies released into the area wasn’t increased in sufficient time when early cases were detected. “We have to take these factors into account, and the long-term management will require coordinated international action rather than isolated local responses,” said Andrés Lira-Noriega , an ecologist studying the interaction between species, including New World screwworm, and their environment at the Institute of Ecology. The blowfly gradually migrated north. “[Mexican officials have] done a great job slowing it down, but there's just not enough flies. One hundred million sounds like a lot, but when you've got to really outnumber the fertile males to stop the population from increasing, that's not enough,” Scott said. On June 3, 2026, the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) announced the first case of New World screwworm in the US in a young calf in Texas. Since then, USDA in collaboration with other agencies in both the US and Mexico have launched responses to track, identify, and mitigate cases of New World screwworm. A new facility to produce up to 300 million sterilized flies per week is under construction in Texas, and a different facility is being retrofitted for this task on a smaller scale in Mexico. While these rapid responses are a commendable example of cooperation and outbreak management, this incident has also provided researchers with an opportunity to consider how the current approaches could be improved. One Health Approaches Can Better Address Complex Problems Chad Cross , a One Health researcher at Texas Tech University, said that, while the sterile insect technique strategy is solid for managing the New World Screwworm, the current outbreak highlights some limitations in the approach. He said that relying on one facility to produce the necessary flies was less than ideal, and that even if it’s not feasible to constantly maintain multiple facilities, having procedures to rapidly increase production early in an outbreak could help. Continue reading below... Article Using Site-Saturation Variant Libraries to Understand Viral Evolution Discover how scientists bridge the gap between genomic detection and functional assessment to remain a step ahead of viral evolution. Read More Additionally, he said that implementing a One Health or multidisciplinary approach could bring more experts to the table to improve surveillance and mitigation. “It is fundamentally a way to de-silo how we approach problems in science,” Cross said. “So instead of looking at it [as] a single basic science pathway towards one part of an answer, we try to develop an integrated set of ways to investigate a problem.” For example, as a coleader of the New World Screwworm response at Texas Tech University where he directs the team’s research efforts, he said that the group has brought in both veterinarians and physicians for educational opportunities. Since these flies can lay eggs in both animal and human tissues, the team wants to make sure that the medical professionals are ready to spot and treat new cases of New World Screwworm. At the same time, they are working with environmental scientists to track cases and develop models to predict where these flies may be most likely to migrate to, which can inform where field personnel need to be sent with interventions. Lira-Noriega said that this is important because researchers cannot physically monitor every location, so predictive models help identify where resources are most needed to respond efficiently. For example, he and his team investigated the factors that influenced where New World Screwworm would likely migrate after the 2022 breach of the Panama barrier. 6 He said that, so far, the spread of the fly has matched his and his team’s predictions, but he added that they found many more areas that have suitable conditions for this fly. He thinks that surveillance and mitigation efforts should extend to these regions as well before cases emerge. “Historically the sterile insect technique has been really useful and is today probably one of the most promising approaches, but definitely it has to be combined with surveillance, rapid response, [and] regional cooperation,” he said. He added that integrating existing tools, such as geospatial information, reported cases, and the distribution of livestock can help public health officials monitor outbreaks in real time and prioritize responses. Lira-Noriega also said that incorporating newer molecular surveillance technologies could further improve surveillance. Cross and his team are working on just those types of solutions. They are collaborating with biochemists and engineers to develop better field tests to detect New World Screwworm, including lateral flow tests to detect the blowfly’s DNA from the wounds of infected animals and strategies to identify the presence of the fly from water trap samples using environmental DNA testing. Male Only Flies: The Next Evolution in the Sterile Insect Technique Another area that researchers have explored improving is in the sterile insect technique itself. Currently, when facilities produce flies, they raise both male and female insects because differentiating them on the scale of millions is not feasible. This leads to raising and releasing female blowflies that don’t largely aid in the elimination strategy, essentially wasting resources. “It's enormously expensive, so that's why people like myself have been searching for more efficient solutions,” Scott said. He and his team developed a male-only line of New World Screwworm flies to overcome this challenge. The researchers use a transgene under the control of tetracycline that is spliced with an intron so that a lethal protein is only overexpressed in female larvae and embryos. 7-9 The Environmental Protection Agency recently chose one of the team’s lines to undergo field testing in Panama. If successful, Scott’s fly line could be approved for use in the US, and with additional regulatory approval, in Mexico. Switching to male-only flies, Scott said, would allow facilities to produce and release more flies that are directly relevant to the mitigation strategy. Additionally, he said that since male flies can be sterilized more easily than female flies, the producers can use lower doses of radiation to perform that task. This reduction could help keep these flies fitter for mating. Looking further ahead, some researchers are exploring avoiding radiation all together. Instead, they hope to use CRISPR-Cas9 -based gene editing to render the flies sterile. Scott said that this strategy would be cheaper. However, researchers currently struggle to grow these colonies at scale, since the approach involves many genetic crosses. One of these strategies or another novel approach could soon get some additional help in coming to fruition. Earlier in the year, the USDA opened a grant application for, in part, novel strategies or enhancements of existing approaches with new technology to improve the control of the New World screwworm. “I suspect we'll find some really neat and inventive molecular techniques being posited as potential solutions to help with the issue,” Cross said. Today, researchers have the tools necessary to combat the New World screwworm, but by working together and implementing new strategies and better technology, scientists and other stakeholders can stay one step ahead of this deadly pest . References Wyss JH. Screwworm eradication in the Americas . Ann NY Acad Sci . 2000;916(1):186-193. Bushland RC, Hopkins DE. Experiments with screwworm flies sterilized by X-rays . J Econ Entomol . 1951;44:(5)725-731. Bushland RC, Hopkins DE. Experiments with screwworm flies sterilized by X-rays and gamma rays . J Econ Entomol . 1953;46(4):648-656. Knipling EF. Possibilities of insect control or eradication through the use of sexually sterile males . J Econ Entomol . 1955;48(4):459-462. Baumhover AH, et al. Screw-worm control through release of sterilized flies . J Econ Entomol . 1955;48(4):462-466. Valdez-Espinosa UM, et al. The reemergence of the New World screwworm and its potential distribution in North America . Sci Rep . 2025;15(1):23819. Concha C, et al. An early female lethal system of the New World screwworm, Cochliomyia hominivorax , for biotechnology-enhanced SIT . BMC Genet . 2020;21(suppl 2):143. Concha C, et al. A transgenic male-only strain of the New World screwworm for an improved control program using the sterile insect technique . BMC Biol . 2016;14(1):72. Li F, et al. Conservation and sex-specific splicing of the transformer g ene in the Calliphorids Cochliomyia hominivorax , Cochliomyia macellaria and Lucilia sericata . PLoS One . 2013;8(2):e56303. Add The Scientist as a preferred Google source to see more of our trusted coverage. Related Topics blowfly , gene drive , gene editing , Genetics , Public Health , sterilization , surveillance Meet the Author Shelby Bradford, PhD Shelby is an Associate Editor at The Scientist. She earned her PhD in immunology and microbial pathogenesis from West Virginia University, where she studied neonatal responses to vaccination. She completed an AAAS Mass Media Fellowship at StateImpact Pennsylvania , and her writing has also appeared in Massive Science. Shelby participated in the 2023 flagship ComSciCon and volunteered with science outreach programs and Carnegie Science Center during graduate school. Share Translational cancer biomarker research helps convert molecular profiles into potentially clinically relevant insights. Achieving this goal requires integrated workflows that connect unbiased discovery technologies with robust analytical validation and confirmatory methods, ranging from broad scope arrays to targeted quantitative tools and beyond. Download this ebook to learn about tools that can assist cancer biomarker researchers every step of the translational journey. Sponsored by Top Image Credit: ©iStock, Jian Fan You might also be interested in... Article Scientists Use Pharmacogenomics to Provide Personalized Medicine By studying genome variants, researchers can identify which drugs are safe and effective for each individual patient. Written by Rebecca Roberts, PhD Rebecca Roberts, PhD Rebecca Roberts is a science writer and communicator. She earned her PhD in molecular biology from the University of the Sunshine Coast in Australia and completed a two-year postdoctoral fellowship at Lund University in Sweden. Her writing focuses on gene editing technology, cell and gene therapies, and the regulatory space. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Dec 20, 2025 | 5 min read Save for Later Using pharmacogenomics, clinicians can make informed decisions about which drug to administer to which patient, and at what dose. Image credit: ©iStock, Utku Yilmazturk PDF version Share Medications do not work the same way for everyone. Many people carry genetic mutations that affect their response to drugs, from painkillers to cancer treatments. Researchers investigate pharmacogenomics to learn how such variations in the genome affect a patient’s therapeutic response. In this article, we delve into the science of pharmacogenomics, how clinicians use it to inform personalized medicine , and some key examples of how pharmacogenomics is applied in clinical settings. What Is Pharmacogenomics? Pharmacogenomics, a portmanteau of pharmacology and genomics sometimes abbreviated as PGx, is the study of how an individual’s genetic variation affects their response to medication. The aim of pharmacogenomic testing is to increase drug efficacy and safety for each unique patient. 1 Scientists use pharmacogenomics as a tool to inform clinical decisions and personalize drug treatments; by studying a person’s genome, scientists can predict if a specific drug will be effective and at what dose, or if the patient is likely to experience adverse drug reactions (ADRs). For example, many individuals have mutations in the cytochrome P450 (CYP) gene family, which encodes liver enzymes responsible for metabolizing a variety of medications. These mutations can reduce CYP enzyme activity, preventing the body from effectively metabolizing and eliminating drugs. Using genome sequencing, clinicians can identify if a patient carries these mutations, reducing the risk of drug-related toxicity or overdose. 2 Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Genetics updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Genes that are known to affect drug responses, like the CYP family, are called pharmacogenes. Because they typically encode drug-metabolizing enzymes, transporters, or receptors, these genes can affect either pharmacokinetics (drug metabolism and transport) or pharmacodynamics (therapeutic actions). 3,4 There is also a growing body of evidence that epigenetic mechanisms can influence drug responses. 4 What is the difference between pharmacogenomics and pharmacogenetics? The terms pharmacogenomics and pharmacogenetics are often used interchangeably by scientists. 5 However, pharmacogenetics may refer to the study of specific genes that affect a person’s ability to metabolize drugs, while pharmacogenomics is a broader term that includes all the possible genomic variations that can affect drug responses. 5 Pharmacogenomic Testing There are several types of pharmacogenomic testing. In concurrent pharmacogenomic testing, clinicians sequence individual target genes after a drug is prescribed but before it is administered to a patient. 6 In contrast, reactive pharmacogenomic testing occurs when a drug has shown limited efficacy in a patient or after an ADR occurs. 7 Pharmacogenomics panels Scientists have developed pharmacogenomics panels that include a wide range of relevant genes that affect common medications. Using these panels, clinicians can perform pharmacogenomic testing pre-emptively, before prescribing certain drugs. 6 Several pharmacogenomics working groups and international consortia have recommended pharmacogenomic panel screening integration into standard healthcare settings. The Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium , for example, has published clinical practice guidelines for more than 100 pharmacogenes. 6 Pharmacogenomics workflow In a standard pharmacogenomics workflow, clinicians use a blood, saliva, or buccal swab sample to isolate DNA from a patient, then perform either targeted genotyping of a specific gene or pharmacogenomics panel, or comprehensive next-generation sequencing. 8,9 Using these data, clinicians can make informed decisions about which drug to recommend to the patient and determine the appropriate dose. Pharmacogenomics helps scientists optimize personalized therapeutic recommendations, such as which medication and dose will provide the best efficacy with the fewest adverse events. modified from ©iStock , VectorMoon , PrettyVectors , natrot Clinical Pharmacogenomics Applications Pharmacogenomic testing for antidepressants Scientists have demonstrated that pharmacogenomic testing can help tailor personalized treatments for depression and anxiety. Genetic variations, including those in the CYP gene family, account for around 60 percent of the variability in response to antidepressant drugs, such as serotonin reuptake inhibitors, serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors, noradrenergic and serotonergic modulators, tricyclic and tetracyclic compounds, and monoamine oxidase inhibitors. 4 Informed by their CYP mutations, patients can be categorized based on whether they will metabolize these drugs slowly, normally, or rapidly; patients who are genetically predisposed to slow therapeutic metabolism are at increased risk of ADRs and therefore require lower dosages, while those with ultrarapid metabolizing enzymes need higher or more frequent dosages for these drugs to be effective. 10 Researchers have also shown that mutations in the monoamine and γ-aminobutyric acid metabolic enzymes, transporters, and receptors; uridine 5’-diphospho-glucuronosyltransferase enzymes; and P-glycoprotein ATP-binding cassette transporters affect antidepressant drug pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics. 4 Continue reading below... Webinar Interpreting CRISPR Screens Through Protein-Level Biology Amanda McQuade and Nathaniel Robichaud discuss how integrated proteomic and transcriptomic profiling can reveal cellular responses to genetic perturbations that are not captured through RNA analysis alone. Read More Cardiovascular pharmacogenomics Cardiovascular disease treatment is another example of how clinicians can apply pharmacogenomics. Warfarin is an anticoagulant that doctors frequently prescribe to patients with cardiovascular disease, including myocardial infarction (heart attack), atrial fibrillation, and thrombosis (blood clots). However, warfarin has a very narrow therapeutic window; excessive anticoagulation can cause dangerous bleeds, while inadequate responses can result in other adverse events. 1 Research has shown that mutations in the CYP 2C9 enzyme and the vitamin K epoxide reductase complex subunit 1 gene can put individual patients at risk of either excessive bleeding while on warfarin, or adverse events, such as heart attack. 1 Based on robust clinical evidence, an international consortium published a pharmacogenomic algorithm that clinicians can implement to determine the correct dosages of warfarin for individual patients. 1 Pharmacogenomics in cancer Pharmacogenomics is also highly applicable in the context of cancer treatment . Many cancer treatments have narrow therapeutic windows and are associated with severe toxicity; using pharmacogenomics, clinicians can tailor cancer therapies to individual patients and avoid potential ADRs. 11 Researchers have identified a range of genetic variants they can use to predict cancer treatment efficacy. This includes mutations in the genes that code for dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPYD), thymidylate synthetase, methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase, thiopurine S-methyltransferase, nucleoside diphosphate-linked moiety X-type motif-15, uridine 5'-diphospho-glucuronosyltransferase, ATP-binding cassettes, and others. 11 For example, mutations in DPYD are associated with severe fluoropyrimidine toxicity that can be fatal. 12 Clinicians can use pharmacogenomics to identify patients that carry these DPYD mutations and significantly reduce the risk of these patients experiencing severe toxicity by reducing the fluoropyrimidine dose by 50 percent. 12 Ethical Issues in Pharmacogenomics Because pharmacogenomics is so effective in determining which drugs and dosages should be administered to patients, researchers and clinicians have proposed that pharmacogenomic testing be widely implemented in standard healthcare setting across the globe. 13 However, other scientists and bioethicists have voiced concerns over potential ethical issues in pharmacogenomics, such as the large-scale collection of genomic data and ownership of such data, and the accessibility of treatments that are inferred from pharmacogenomic data. 13 Future Outlooks As researchers continue to learn more about how the genome affects drug responses and pharmacogenomics becomes integrated into standard healthcare, patients will hopefully benefit from more personalized treatment plans. By increasing the likelihood of drug efficacy and minimizing the risk of ADRs, pharmacogenomics could reduce the burden on global healthcare systems. References Ross S, et al. News As the New World Screwworm Returns, Scientists Unite to Fight Back As local and federal authorities respond to the current outbreak of the flesh-eating blowfly, researchers look ahead to new approaches to keep New World screwworm at bay. Written by Shelby Bradford, PhD Shelby Bradford, PhD Shelby is an Assistant Editor for The Scientist . She earned her PhD from West Virginia University in immunology and microbiology and completed an AAAS Mass Media fellowship. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Jun 19, 2026 | 6 min read Save for Later A blowfly that lays its eggs in wounds of live animals poses major health and safety risks. Image credit: ©iStock.com, Lidya Elfa Sari PDF version Register for free to listen to this article Listen with   Speechify 0:00 6:00 Share I nsect pests like flies are a nuisance, but some, like the New World screwworm, have the potential to devastate cattle, a serious and costly bane to the agriculture industry. The New World screwworm is a type of blowfly that sniffs out and lays eggs in the wounds of living tissue, often livestock and companion animals. When the larvae emerge, they eat the surrounding flesh before falling out to mature in the soil, causing more injury that attracts more blowflies to the site in the meantime. Before mitigation efforts, New World screwworm led to more than $200 million in livestock losses every year in the US alone. 1 While strategies to eliminate New World screwworm populations have worked well in the past, today, researchers are exploring new genetic technologies and multidisciplinary approaches to better combat this devastating pest. Sterile Insects Kept New World Screwworm at Bay The New World screwworm is endemic to the warm regions of the Americas. To combat this devastating fly, scientists took advantage of the insect’s sexual behavior. 2-4 Male blowflies mate aggressively, while female blowflies only mate once. By sterilizing large numbers of flies to release them into the wild, researchers discovered that they could drive out the blowflies by causing large numbers of unsuccessful pairings. 5 Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Immunology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe “It was a brilliant idea,” said Maxwell Scott , an entomologist at North Carolina State University. Indeed, the US in coordination with Mexico and, later, central American countries drove the New World screwworm out of their territories down to Panama in the 1960s. There, a production facility has continued to churn out up to 100 million flies a week to maintain a barrier between Panama and Colombia. “So, it can be very effective, but it is a numbers game. You've got to outnumber the fertile flies in the area,” Scott said. However, in 2022, the barricade broke. Researchers said that it was likely a combination of many factors: Varying land use had introduced more cattle into the area, the COVID-19 pandemic caused a decrease in the number of people available to conduct investigations, and the number of flies released into the area wasn’t increased in sufficient time when early cases were detected. “We have to take these factors into account, and the long-term management will require coordinated international action rather than isolated local responses,” said Andrés Lira-Noriega , an ecologist studying the interaction between species, including New World screwworm, and their environment at the Institute of Ecology. The blowfly gradually migrated north. “[Mexican officials have] done a great job slowing it down, but there's just not enough flies. One hundred million sounds like a lot, but when you've got to really outnumber the fertile males to stop the population from increasing, that's not enough,” Scott said. On June 3, 2026, the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) announced the first case of New World screwworm in the US in a young calf in Texas. Since then, USDA in collaboration with other agencies in both the US and Mexico have launched responses to track, identify, and mitigate cases of New World screwworm. A new facility to produce up to 300 million sterilized flies per week is under construction in Texas, and a different facility is being retrofitted for this task on a smaller scale in Mexico. While these rapid responses are a commendable example of cooperation and outbreak management, this incident has also provided researchers with an opportunity to consider how the current approaches could be improved. One Health Approaches Can Better Address Complex Problems Chad Cross , a One Health researcher at Texas Tech University, said that, while the sterile insect technique strategy is solid for managing the New World Screwworm, the current outbreak highlights some limitations in the approach. He said that relying on one facility to produce the necessary flies was less than ideal, and that even if it’s not feasible to constantly maintain multiple facilities, having procedures to rapidly increase production early in an outbreak could help. Continue reading below... Article Using Site-Saturation Variant Libraries to Understand Viral Evolution Discover how scientists bridge the gap between genomic detection and functional assessment to remain a step ahead of viral evolution. Read More Additionally, he said that implementing a One Health or multidisciplinary approach could bring more experts to the table to improve surveillance and mitigation. “It is fundamentally a way to de-silo how we approach problems in science,” Cross said. “So instead of looking at it [as] a single basic science pathway towards one part of an answer, we try to develop an integrated set of ways to investigate a problem.” For example, as a coleader of the New World Screwworm response at Texas Tech University where he directs the team’s research efforts, he said that the group has brought in both veterinarians and physicians for educational opportunities. Since these flies can lay eggs in both animal and human tissues, the team wants to make sure that the medical professionals are ready to spot and treat new cases of New World Screwworm. At the same time, they are working with environmental scientists to track cases and develop models to predict where these flies may be most likely to migrate to, which can inform where field personnel need to be sent with interventions. Lira-Noriega said that this is important because researchers cannot physically monitor every location, so predictive models help identify where resources are most needed to respond efficiently. For example, he and his team investigated the factors that influenced where New World Screwworm would likely migrate after the 2022 breach of the Panama barrier. 6 He said that, so far, the spread of the fly has matched his and his team’s predictions, but he added that they found many more areas that have suitable conditions for this fly. He thinks that surveillance and mitigation efforts should extend to these regions as well before cases emerge. “Historically the sterile insect technique has been really useful and is today probably one of the most promising approaches, but definitely it has to be combined with surveillance, rapid response, [and] regional cooperation,” he said. He added that integrating existing tools, such as geospatial information, reported cases, and the distribution of livestock can help public health officials monitor outbreaks in real time and prioritize responses. Lira-Noriega also said that incorporating newer molecular surveillance technologies could further improve surveillance. Cross and his team are working on just those types of solutions. They are collaborating with biochemists and engineers to develop better field tests to detect New World Screwworm, including lateral flow tests to detect the blowfly’s DNA from the wounds of infected animals and strategies to identify the presence of the fly from water trap samples using environmental DNA testing. Male Only Flies: The Next Evolution in the Sterile Insect Technique Another area that researchers have explored improving is in the sterile insect technique itself. Currently, when facilities produce flies, they raise both male and female insects because differentiating them on the scale of millions is not feasible. This leads to raising and releasing female blowflies that don’t largely aid in the elimination strategy, essentially wasting resources. “It's enormously expensive, so that's why people like myself have been searching for more efficient solutions,” Scott said. He and his team developed a male-only line of New World Screwworm flies to overcome this challenge. The researchers use a transgene under the control of tetracycline that is spliced with an intron so that a lethal protein is only overexpressed in female larvae and embryos. 7-9 The Environmental Protection Agency recently chose one of the team’s lines to undergo field testing in Panama. If successful, Scott’s fly line could be approved for use in the US, and with additional regulatory approval, in Mexico. Switching to male-only flies, Scott said, would allow facilities to produce and release more flies that are directly relevant to the mitigation strategy. Additionally, he said that since male flies can be sterilized more easily than female flies, the producers can use lower doses of radiation to perform that task. This reduction could help keep these flies fitter for mating. Looking further ahead, some researchers are exploring avoiding radiation all together. Instead, they hope to use CRISPR-Cas9 -based gene editing to render the flies sterile. Scott said that this strategy would be cheaper. However, researchers currently struggle to grow these colonies at scale, since the approach involves many genetic crosses. One of these strategies or another novel approach could soon get some additional help in coming to fruition. Earlier in the year, the USDA opened a grant application for, in part, novel strategies or enhancements of existing approaches with new technology to improve the control of the New World screwworm. “I suspect we'll find some really neat and inventive molecular techniques being posited as potential solutions to help with the issue,” Cross said. Today, researchers have the tools necessary to combat the New World screwworm, but by working together and implementing new strategies and better technology, scientists and other stakeholders can stay one step ahead of this deadly pest . Article Scientists Use Pharmacogenomics to Provide Personalized Medicine By studying genome variants, researchers can identify which drugs are safe and effective for each individual patient. Written by Rebecca Roberts, PhD Rebecca Roberts, PhD Rebecca Roberts is a science writer and communicator. She earned her PhD in molecular biology from the University of the Sunshine Coast in Australia and completed a two-year postdoctoral fellowship at Lund University in Sweden. Her writing focuses on gene editing technology, cell and gene therapies, and the regulatory space. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Dec 20, 2025 | 5 min read Save for Later Using pharmacogenomics, clinicians can make informed decisions about which drug to administer to which patient, and at what dose. Image credit: ©iStock, Utku Yilmazturk PDF version Share Medications do not work the same way for everyone. Many people carry genetic mutations that affect their response to drugs, from painkillers to cancer treatments. Researchers investigate pharmacogenomics to learn how such variations in the genome affect a patient’s therapeutic response. In this article, we delve into the science of pharmacogenomics, how clinicians use it to inform personalized medicine , and some key examples of how pharmacogenomics is applied in clinical settings. What Is Pharmacogenomics? Pharmacogenomics, a portmanteau of pharmacology and genomics sometimes abbreviated as PGx, is the study of how an individual’s genetic variation affects their response to medication. The aim of pharmacogenomic testing is to increase drug efficacy and safety for each unique patient. 1 Scientists use pharmacogenomics as a tool to inform clinical decisions and personalize drug treatments; by studying a person’s genome, scientists can predict if a specific drug will be effective and at what dose, or if the patient is likely to experience adverse drug reactions (ADRs). For example, many individuals have mutations in the cytochrome P450 (CYP) gene family, which encodes liver enzymes responsible for metabolizing a variety of medications. These mutations can reduce CYP enzyme activity, preventing the body from effectively metabolizing and eliminating drugs. Using genome sequencing, clinicians can identify if a patient carries these mutations, reducing the risk of drug-related toxicity or overdose. 2 Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Genetics updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Genes that are known to affect drug responses, like the CYP family, are called pharmacogenes. Because they typically encode drug-metabolizing enzymes, transporters, or receptors, these genes can affect either pharmacokinetics (drug metabolism and transport) or pharmacodynamics (therapeutic actions). 3,4 There is also a growing body of evidence that epigenetic mechanisms can influence drug responses. 4 What is the difference between pharmacogenomics and pharmacogenetics? The terms pharmacogenomics and pharmacogenetics are often used interchangeably by scientists. 5 However, pharmacogenetics may refer to the study of specific genes that affect a person’s ability to metabolize drugs, while pharmacogenomics is a broader term that includes all the possible genomic variations that can affect drug responses. 5 Pharmacogenomic Testing There are several types of pharmacogenomic testing. In concurrent pharmacogenomic testing, clinicians sequence individual target genes after a drug is prescribed but before it is administered to a patient. 6 In contrast, reactive pharmacogenomic testing occurs when a drug has shown limited efficacy in a patient or after an ADR occurs. 7 Pharmacogenomics panels Scientists have developed pharmacogenomics panels that include a wide range of relevant genes that affect common medications. Using these panels, clinicians can perform pharmacogenomic testing pre-emptively, before prescribing certain drugs. 6 Several pharmacogenomics working groups and international consortia have recommended pharmacogenomic panel screening integration into standard healthcare settings. The Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium , for example, has published clinical practice guidelines for more than 100 pharmacogenes. 6 Pharmacogenomics workflow In a standard pharmacogenomics workflow, clinicians use a blood, saliva, or buccal swab sample to isolate DNA from a patient, then perform either targeted genotyping of a specific gene or pharmacogenomics panel, or comprehensive next-generation sequencing. 8,9 Using these data, clinicians can make informed decisions about which drug to recommend to the patient and determine the appropriate dose. Pharmacogenomics helps scientists optimize personalized therapeutic recommendations, such as which medication and dose will provide the best efficacy with the fewest adverse events. modified from ©iStock , VectorMoon , PrettyVectors , natrot Clinical Pharmacogenomics Applications Pharmacogenomic testing for antidepressants Scientists have demonstrated that pharmacogenomic testing can help tailor personalized treatments for depression and anxiety. Genetic variations, including those in the CYP gene family, account for around 60 percent of the variability in response to antidepressant drugs, such as serotonin reuptake inhibitors, serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors, noradrenergic and serotonergic modulators, tricyclic and tetracyclic compounds, and monoamine oxidase inhibitors. 4 Informed by their CYP mutations, patients can be categorized based on whether they will metabolize these drugs slowly, normally, or rapidly; patients who are genetically predisposed to slow therapeutic metabolism are at increased risk of ADRs and therefore require lower dosages, while those with ultrarapid metabolizing enzymes need higher or more frequent dosages for these drugs to be effective. 10 Researchers have also shown that mutations in the monoamine and γ-aminobutyric acid metabolic enzymes, transporters, and receptors; uridine 5’-diphospho-glucuronosyltransferase enzymes; and P-glycoprotein ATP-binding cassette transporters affect antidepressant drug pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics. 4 Continue reading below... Webinar Interpreting CRISPR Screens Through Protein-Level Biology Amanda McQuade and Nathaniel Robichaud discuss how integrated proteomic and transcriptomic profiling can reveal cellular responses to genetic perturbations that are not captured through RNA analysis alone. Read More Cardiovascular pharmacogenomics Cardiovascular disease treatment is another example of how clinicians can apply pharmacogenomics. Warfarin is an anticoagulant that doctors frequently prescribe to patients with cardiovascular disease, including myocardial infarction (heart attack), atrial fibrillation, and thrombosis (blood clots). However, warfarin has a very narrow therapeutic window; excessive anticoagulation can cause dangerous bleeds, while inadequate responses can result in other adverse events. 1 Research has shown that mutations in the CYP 2C9 enzyme and the vitamin K epoxide reductase complex subunit 1 gene can put individual patients at risk of either excessive bleeding while on warfarin, or adverse events, such as heart attack. 1 Based on robust clinical evidence, an international consortium published a pharmacogenomic algorithm that clinicians can implement to determine the correct dosages of warfarin for individual patients. 1 Pharmacogenomics in cancer Pharmacogenomics is also highly applicable in the context of cancer treatment . Many cancer treatments have narrow therapeutic windows and are associated with severe toxicity; using pharmacogenomics, clinicians can tailor cancer therapies to individual patients and avoid potential ADRs. 11 Researchers have identified a range of genetic variants they can use to predict cancer treatment efficacy. This includes mutations in the genes that code for dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPYD), thymidylate synthetase, methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase, thiopurine S-methyltransferase, nucleoside diphosphate-linked moiety X-type motif-15, uridine 5'-diphospho-glucuronosyltransferase, ATP-binding cassettes, and others. 11 For example, mutations in DPYD are associated with severe fluoropyrimidine toxicity that can be fatal. 12 Clinicians can use pharmacogenomics to identify patients that carry these DPYD mutations and significantly reduce the risk of these patients experiencing severe toxicity by reducing the fluoropyrimidine dose by 50 percent. 12 Ethical Issues in Pharmacogenomics Because pharmacogenomics is so effective in determining which drugs and dosages should be administered to patients, researchers and clinicians have proposed that pharmacogenomic testing be widely implemented in standard healthcare setting across the globe. 13 However, other scientists and bioethicists have voiced concerns over potential ethical issues in pharmacogenomics, such as the large-scale collection of genomic data and ownership of such data, and the accessibility of treatments that are inferred from pharmacogenomic data. 13 Future Outlooks As researchers continue to learn more about how the genome affects drug responses and pharmacogenomics becomes integrated into standard healthcare, patients will hopefully benefit from more personalized treatment plans. By increasing the likelihood of drug efficacy and minimizing the risk of ADRs, pharmacogenomics could reduce the burden on global healthcare systems. References Ross S, et al. The blowfly gradually migrated north. “[Mexican officials have] done a great job slowing it down, but there's just not enough flies. One hundred million sounds like a lot, but when you've got to really outnumber the fertile males to stop the population from increasing, that's not enough,” Scott said. On June 3, 2026, the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) announced the first case of New World screwworm in the US in a young calf in Texas. Since then, USDA in collaboration with other agencies in both the US and Mexico have launched responses to track, identify, and mitigate cases of New World screwworm. A new facility to produce up to 300 million sterilized flies per week is under construction in Texas, and a different facility is being retrofitted for this task on a smaller scale in Mexico. While these rapid responses are a commendable example of cooperation and outbreak management, this incident has also provided researchers with an opportunity to consider how the current approaches could be improved. One Health Approaches Can Better Address Complex Problems Chad Cross , a One Health researcher at Texas Tech University, said that, while the sterile insect technique strategy is solid for managing the New World Screwworm, the current outbreak highlights some limitations in the approach. He said that relying on one facility to produce the necessary flies was less than ideal, and that even if it’s not feasible to constantly maintain multiple facilities, having procedures to rapidly increase production early in an outbreak could help. Continue reading below... Article Using Site-Saturation Variant Libraries to Understand Viral Evolution Discover how scientists bridge the gap between genomic detection and functional assessment to remain a step ahead of viral evolution. Read More Additionally, he said that implementing a One Health or multidisciplinary approach could bring more experts to the table to improve surveillance and mitigation. “It is fundamentally a way to de-silo how we approach problems in science,” Cross said. “So instead of looking at it [as] a single basic science pathway towards one part of an answer, we try to develop an integrated set of ways to investigate a problem.” For example, as a coleader of the New World Screwworm response at Texas Tech University where he directs the team’s research efforts, he said that the group has brought in both veterinarians and physicians for educational opportunities. Since these flies can lay eggs in both animal and human tissues, the team wants to make sure that the medical professionals are ready to spot and treat new cases of New World Screwworm. At the same time, they are working with environmental scientists to track cases and develop models to predict where these flies may be most likely to migrate to, which can inform where field personnel need to be sent with interventions. Lira-Noriega said that this is important because researchers cannot physically monitor every location, so predictive models help identify where resources are most needed to respond efficiently. For example, he and his team investigated the factors that influenced where New World Screwworm would likely migrate after the 2022 breach of the Panama barrier. 6 He said that, so far, the spread of the fly has matched his and his team’s predictions, but he added that they found many more areas that have suitable conditions for this fly. He thinks that surveillance and mitigation efforts should extend to these regions as well before cases emerge. “Historically the sterile insect technique has been really useful and is today probably one of the most promising approaches, but definitely it has to be combined with surveillance, rapid response, [and] regional cooperation,” he said. He added that integrating existing tools, such as geospatial information, reported cases, and the distribution of livestock can help public health officials monitor outbreaks in real time and prioritize responses. Lira-Noriega also said that incorporating newer molecular surveillance technologies could further improve surveillance. Cross and his team are working on just those types of solutions. They are collaborating with biochemists and engineers to develop better field tests to detect New World Screwworm, including lateral flow tests to detect the blowfly’s DNA from the wounds of infected animals and strategies to identify the presence of the fly from water trap samples using environmental DNA testing. Male Only Flies: The Next Evolution in the Sterile Insect Technique Another area that researchers have explored improving is in the sterile insect technique itself. Currently, when facilities produce flies, they raise both male and female insects because differentiating them on the scale of millions is not feasible. This leads to raising and releasing female blowflies that don’t largely aid in the elimination strategy, essentially wasting resources. “It's enormously expensive, so that's why people like myself have been searching for more efficient solutions,” Scott said. He and his team developed a male-only line of New World Screwworm flies to overcome this challenge. The researchers use a transgene under the control of tetracycline that is spliced with an intron so that a lethal protein is only overexpressed in female larvae and embryos. 7-9 The Environmental Protection Agency recently chose one of the team’s lines to undergo field testing in Panama. If successful, Scott’s fly line could be approved for use in the US, and with additional regulatory approval, in Mexico. Switching to male-only flies, Scott said, would allow facilities to produce and release more flies that are directly relevant to the mitigation strategy. Additionally, he said that since male flies can be sterilized more easily than female flies, the producers can use lower doses of radiation to perform that task. This reduction could help keep these flies fitter for mating. Looking further ahead, some researchers are exploring avoiding radiation all together. Instead, they hope to use CRISPR-Cas9 -based gene editing to render the flies sterile. Scott said that this strategy would be cheaper. However, researchers currently struggle to grow these colonies at scale, since the approach involves many genetic crosses. One of these strategies or another novel approach could soon get some additional help in coming to fruition. Earlier in the year, the USDA opened a grant application for, in part, novel strategies or enhancements of existing approaches with new technology to improve the control of the New World screwworm. “I suspect we'll find some really neat and inventive molecular techniques being posited as potential solutions to help with the issue,” Cross said. Today, researchers have the tools necessary to combat the New World screwworm, but by working together and implementing new strategies and better technology, scientists and other stakeholders can stay one step ahead of this deadly pest . By sterilizing large numbers of flies to release them into the wild, researchers discovered that they could drive out the blowflies by causing large numbers of unsuccessful pairings. 5 Continue reading below... The New World screwworm is endemic to the warm regions of the Americas. To combat this devastating fly, scientists took advantage of the insect’s sexual behavior. 2-4 Male blowflies mate aggressively, while female blowflies only mate once. By sterilizing large numbers of flies to release them into the wild, researchers discovered that they could drive out the blowflies by causing large numbers of unsuccessful pairings. 5
2-4
5

“It was a brilliant idea,” said Maxwell Scott , an entomologist at North Carolina State University. Indeed, the US in coordination with Mexico and, later, central American countries drove the New World screwworm out of their territories down to Panama in the 1960s. There, a production facility has continued to churn out up to 100 million flies a week to maintain a barrier between Panama and Colombia. “So, it can be very effective, but it is a numbers game. You've got to outnumber the fertile flies in the area,” Scott said.
Maxwell Scott
However, in 2022, the barricade broke. Researchers said that it was likely a combination of many factors: Varying land use had introduced more cattle into the area, the COVID-19 pandemic caused a decrease in the number of people available to conduct investigations, and the number of flies released into the area wasn’t increased in sufficient time when early cases were detected.
“We have to take these factors into account, and the long-term management will require coordinated international action rather than isolated local responses,” said Andrés Lira-Noriega , an ecologist studying the interaction between species, including New World screwworm, and their environment at the Institute of Ecology.
Andrés Lira-Noriega
The blowfly gradually migrated north. “[Mexican officials have] done a great job slowing it down, but there's just not enough flies. One hundred million sounds like a lot, but when you've got to really outnumber the fertile males to stop the population from increasing, that's not enough,” Scott said.
On June 3, 2026, the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) announced the first case of New World screwworm in the US in a young calf in Texas. Since then, USDA in collaboration with other agencies in both the US and Mexico have launched responses to track, identify, and mitigate cases of New World screwworm. A new facility to produce up to 300 million sterilized flies per week is under construction in Texas, and a different facility is being retrofitted for this task on a smaller scale in Mexico.
first case of New World screwworm
While these rapid responses are a commendable example of cooperation and outbreak management, this incident has also provided researchers with an opportunity to consider how the current approaches could be improved.
One Health Approaches Can Better Address Complex Problems
Chad Cross , a One Health researcher at Texas Tech University, said that, while the sterile insect technique strategy is solid for managing the New World Screwworm, the current outbreak highlights some limitations in the approach. He said that relying on one facility to produce the necessary flies was less than ideal, and that even if it’s not feasible to constantly maintain multiple facilities, having procedures to rapidly increase production early in an outbreak could help.
Chad Cross
outbreak

Additionally, he said that implementing a One Health or multidisciplinary approach could bring more experts to the table to improve surveillance and mitigation. “It is fundamentally a way to de-silo how we approach problems in science,” Cross said. “So instead of looking at it [as] a single basic science pathway towards one part of an answer, we try to develop an integrated set of ways to investigate a problem.”
For example, as a coleader of the New World Screwworm response at Texas Tech University where he directs the team’s research efforts, he said that the group has brought in both veterinarians and physicians for educational opportunities. Since these flies can lay eggs in both animal and human tissues, the team wants to make sure that the medical professionals are ready to spot and treat new cases of New World Screwworm. At the same time, they are working with environmental scientists to track cases and develop models to predict where these flies may be most likely to migrate to, which can inform where field personnel need to be sent with interventions.
Lira-Noriega said that this is important because researchers cannot physically monitor every location, so predictive models help identify where resources are most needed to respond efficiently. For example, he and his team investigated the factors that influenced where New World Screwworm would likely migrate after the 2022 breach of the Panama barrier. 6 He said that, so far, the spread of the fly has matched his and his team’s predictions, but he added that they found many more areas that have suitable conditions for this fly. He thinks that surveillance and mitigation efforts should extend to these regions as well before cases emerge.
6
“Historically the sterile insect technique has been really useful and is today probably one of the most promising approaches, but definitely it has to be combined with surveillance, rapid response, [and] regional cooperation,” he said. He added that integrating existing tools, such as geospatial information, reported cases, and the distribution of livestock can help public health officials monitor outbreaks in real time and prioritize responses. Lira-Noriega also said that incorporating newer molecular surveillance technologies could further improve surveillance.
Cross and his team are working on just those types of solutions. They are collaborating with biochemists and engineers to develop better field tests to detect New World Screwworm, including lateral flow tests to detect the blowfly’s DNA from the wounds of infected animals and strategies to identify the presence of the fly from water trap samples using environmental DNA testing.
Male Only Flies: The Next Evolution in the Sterile Insect Technique
Another area that researchers have explored improving is in the sterile insect technique itself. Currently, when facilities produce flies, they raise both male and female insects because differentiating them on the scale of millions is not feasible. This leads to raising and releasing female blowflies that don’t largely aid in the elimination strategy, essentially wasting resources.
sterile insect technique
“It's enormously expensive, so that's why people like myself have been searching for more efficient solutions,” Scott said. He and his team developed a male-only line of New World Screwworm flies to overcome this challenge. The researchers use a transgene under the control of tetracycline that is spliced with an intron so that a lethal protein is only overexpressed in female larvae and embryos. 7-9
7-9
The Environmental Protection Agency recently chose one of the team’s lines to undergo field testing in Panama. If successful, Scott’s fly line could be approved for use in the US, and with additional regulatory approval, in Mexico.
Switching to male-only flies, Scott said, would allow facilities to produce and release more flies that are directly relevant to the mitigation strategy. Additionally, he said that since male flies can be sterilized more easily than female flies, the producers can use lower doses of radiation to perform that task. This reduction could help keep these flies fitter for mating.
Looking further ahead, some researchers are exploring avoiding radiation all together. Instead, they hope to use CRISPR-Cas9 -based gene editing to render the flies sterile. Scott said that this strategy would be cheaper. However, researchers currently struggle to grow these colonies at scale, since the approach involves many genetic crosses.
CRISPR-Cas9
One of these strategies or another novel approach could soon get some additional help in coming to fruition. Earlier in the year, the USDA opened a grant application for, in part, novel strategies or enhancements of existing approaches with new technology to improve the control of the New World screwworm.
grant application
“I suspect we'll find some really neat and inventive molecular techniques being posited as potential solutions to help with the issue,” Cross said.
Today, researchers have the tools necessary to combat the New World screwworm, but by working together and implementing new strategies and better technology, scientists and other stakeholders can stay one step ahead of this deadly pest .
pest

Article Scientists Use Pharmacogenomics to Provide Personalized Medicine By studying genome variants, researchers can identify which drugs are safe and effective for each individual patient. Written by Rebecca Roberts, PhD Rebecca Roberts, PhD Rebecca Roberts is a science writer and communicator. She earned her PhD in molecular biology from the University of the Sunshine Coast in Australia and completed a two-year postdoctoral fellowship at Lund University in Sweden. Her writing focuses on gene editing technology, cell and gene therapies, and the regulatory space. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Dec 20, 2025 | 5 min read Save for Later Using pharmacogenomics, clinicians can make informed decisions about which drug to administer to which patient, and at what dose. Image credit: ©iStock, Utku Yilmazturk PDF version Share Medications do not work the same way for everyone. Many people carry genetic mutations that affect their response to drugs, from painkillers to cancer treatments. Researchers investigate pharmacogenomics to learn how such variations in the genome affect a patient’s therapeutic response. In this article, we delve into the science of pharmacogenomics, how clinicians use it to inform personalized medicine , and some key examples of how pharmacogenomics is applied in clinical settings. What Is Pharmacogenomics? Pharmacogenomics, a portmanteau of pharmacology and genomics sometimes abbreviated as PGx, is the study of how an individual’s genetic variation affects their response to medication. The aim of pharmacogenomic testing is to increase drug efficacy and safety for each unique patient. 1 Scientists use pharmacogenomics as a tool to inform clinical decisions and personalize drug treatments; by studying a person’s genome, scientists can predict if a specific drug will be effective and at what dose, or if the patient is likely to experience adverse drug reactions (ADRs). For example, many individuals have mutations in the cytochrome P450 (CYP) gene family, which encodes liver enzymes responsible for metabolizing a variety of medications. These mutations can reduce CYP enzyme activity, preventing the body from effectively metabolizing and eliminating drugs. Using genome sequencing, clinicians can identify if a patient carries these mutations, reducing the risk of drug-related toxicity or overdose. 2 Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Genetics updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Genes that are known to affect drug responses, like the CYP family, are called pharmacogenes. Because they typically encode drug-metabolizing enzymes, transporters, or receptors, these genes can affect either pharmacokinetics (drug metabolism and transport) or pharmacodynamics (therapeutic actions). 3,4 There is also a growing body of evidence that epigenetic mechanisms can influence drug responses. 4 What is the difference between pharmacogenomics and pharmacogenetics? The terms pharmacogenomics and pharmacogenetics are often used interchangeably by scientists. 5 However, pharmacogenetics may refer to the study of specific genes that affect a person’s ability to metabolize drugs, while pharmacogenomics is a broader term that includes all the possible genomic variations that can affect drug responses. 5 Pharmacogenomic Testing There are several types of pharmacogenomic testing. In concurrent pharmacogenomic testing, clinicians sequence individual target genes after a drug is prescribed but before it is administered to a patient. 6 In contrast, reactive pharmacogenomic testing occurs when a drug has shown limited efficacy in a patient or after an ADR occurs. 7 Pharmacogenomics panels Scientists have developed pharmacogenomics panels that include a wide range of relevant genes that affect common medications. Using these panels, clinicians can perform pharmacogenomic testing pre-emptively, before prescribing certain drugs. 6 Several pharmacogenomics working groups and international consortia have recommended pharmacogenomic panel screening integration into standard healthcare settings. The Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium , for example, has published clinical practice guidelines for more than 100 pharmacogenes. 6 Pharmacogenomics workflow In a standard pharmacogenomics workflow, clinicians use a blood, saliva, or buccal swab sample to isolate DNA from a patient, then perform either targeted genotyping of a specific gene or pharmacogenomics panel, or comprehensive next-generation sequencing. 8,9 Using these data, clinicians can make informed decisions about which drug to recommend to the patient and determine the appropriate dose. Pharmacogenomics helps scientists optimize personalized therapeutic recommendations, such as which medication and dose will provide the best efficacy with the fewest adverse events. modified from ©iStock , VectorMoon , PrettyVectors , natrot Clinical Pharmacogenomics Applications Pharmacogenomic testing for antidepressants Scientists have demonstrated that pharmacogenomic testing can help tailor personalized treatments for depression and anxiety. Genetic variations, including those in the CYP gene family, account for around 60 percent of the variability in response to antidepressant drugs, such as serotonin reuptake inhibitors, serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors, noradrenergic and serotonergic modulators, tricyclic and tetracyclic compounds, and monoamine oxidase inhibitors. 4 Informed by their CYP mutations, patients can be categorized based on whether they will metabolize these drugs slowly, normally, or rapidly; patients who are genetically predisposed to slow therapeutic metabolism are at increased risk of ADRs and therefore require lower dosages, while those with ultrarapid metabolizing enzymes need higher or more frequent dosages for these drugs to be effective. 10 Researchers have also shown that mutations in the monoamine and γ-aminobutyric acid metabolic enzymes, transporters, and receptors; uridine 5’-diphospho-glucuronosyltransferase enzymes; and P-glycoprotein ATP-binding cassette transporters affect antidepressant drug pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics. 4 Continue reading below... Webinar Interpreting CRISPR Screens Through Protein-Level Biology Amanda McQuade and Nathaniel Robichaud discuss how integrated proteomic and transcriptomic profiling can reveal cellular responses to genetic perturbations that are not captured through RNA analysis alone. Read More Cardiovascular pharmacogenomics Cardiovascular disease treatment is another example of how clinicians can apply pharmacogenomics. Warfarin is an anticoagulant that doctors frequently prescribe to patients with cardiovascular disease, including myocardial infarction (heart attack), atrial fibrillation, and thrombosis (blood clots). However, warfarin has a very narrow therapeutic window; excessive anticoagulation can cause dangerous bleeds, while inadequate responses can result in other adverse events. 1 Research has shown that mutations in the CYP 2C9 enzyme and the vitamin K epoxide reductase complex subunit 1 gene can put individual patients at risk of either excessive bleeding while on warfarin, or adverse events, such as heart attack. 1 Based on robust clinical evidence, an international consortium published a pharmacogenomic algorithm that clinicians can implement to determine the correct dosages of warfarin for individual patients. 1 Pharmacogenomics in cancer Pharmacogenomics is also highly applicable in the context of cancer treatment . Many cancer treatments have narrow therapeutic windows and are associated with severe toxicity; using pharmacogenomics, clinicians can tailor cancer therapies to individual patients and avoid potential ADRs. 11 Researchers have identified a range of genetic variants they can use to predict cancer treatment efficacy. This includes mutations in the genes that code for dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPYD), thymidylate synthetase, methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase, thiopurine S-methyltransferase, nucleoside diphosphate-linked moiety X-type motif-15, uridine 5'-diphospho-glucuronosyltransferase, ATP-binding cassettes, and others. 11 For example, mutations in DPYD are associated with severe fluoropyrimidine toxicity that can be fatal. 12 Clinicians can use pharmacogenomics to identify patients that carry these DPYD mutations and significantly reduce the risk of these patients experiencing severe toxicity by reducing the fluoropyrimidine dose by 50 percent. 12 Ethical Issues in Pharmacogenomics Because pharmacogenomics is so effective in determining which drugs and dosages should be administered to patients, researchers and clinicians have proposed that pharmacogenomic testing be widely implemented in standard healthcare setting across the globe. 13 However, other scientists and bioethicists have voiced concerns over potential ethical issues in pharmacogenomics, such as the large-scale collection of genomic data and ownership of such data, and the accessibility of treatments that are inferred from pharmacogenomic data. 13 Future Outlooks As researchers continue to learn more about how the genome affects drug responses and pharmacogenomics becomes integrated into standard healthcare, patients will hopefully benefit from more personalized treatment plans. By increasing the likelihood of drug efficacy and minimizing the risk of ADRs, pharmacogenomics could reduce the burden on global healthcare systems. References Ross S, et al.
